records.ety: [OF. conzoivre, concever, conceveir, F. concevoir, fr. L. oncipere to take, to conceive; con- + capere to seize or take. See Capable, and cf. Conception.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Conceived (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Conceiving.]

defs: To receive into the womb and begin to breed; to begin the formation of the embryo of.
quotes: She hath also conceived a son in her old age.

defs: To form in the mind; to plan; to devise; to generate; to originate; as, to conceive a purpose, plan, hope.
quotes: It was among the ruins of the Capitol that I first conceived the idea of a work which has amused and exercised near twenty years of my life.
Conceiving and uttering from the heart words of falsehood.

defs: To apprehend by reason or imagination; to take into the mind; to know; to imagine; to comprehend; to understand.
quotes: O horror, horror, horror! Tongue nor heart Cannot conceive nor name thee!
You will hardly conceive him to have been bred in the same climate.

defs: To have an embryo or fetus formed in the womb; to breed; to become pregnant.
quotes: A virgin shall conceive, and bear a son.

defs: To have a conception, idea, or opinion; think; -- with of.
quotes: Conceive of things clearly and distinctly in their own natures.
